title: Basic word order (SVO) and no inflection
explanationMd: Vietnamese is an **analytic** language: words never change shape. There is **no verb conjugation, no plurals, no articles (a/the), and no grammatical gender**. The core order is **Subject – Verb – Object**, just like English: **Tôi ăn cơm** (I eat rice). One form of a word covers every person and tense — **ăn** means eat / eats / ate / eaten. Meaning comes from word order and from separate helper words (đã, đang, sẽ...), not from endings. The plural is understood from context or from words like **các** / **những**, never from the noun itself.

commonErrors.note: Verbs never take endings — do not add -s or any suffix.
Nouns have no plural -s; leave the noun bare.

title: Saying 'I': tôi and mình
explanationMd: Vietnamese has no single neutral word for **I**; you pick one that fits the relationship. The safe default with strangers, in class, or in any neutral situation is **tôi** (I). Among close friends or with a partner, people use **mình** (I / me, intimate). **Mình** can also mean **we** and is used to address a spouse affectionately. Start with **tôi** everywhere until you know the relationship — it is polite and never wrong for a beginner. Note that in warm, family-style conversation, native speakers often replace even **tôi** with a kinship term for 'I' (see the kinship entries).

examples.translation: I'll go first, okay. (to a friend)

commonErrors.wrong: Mình là bác sĩ. (to your new boss)
commonErrors.note: Mình is intimate; use tôi with people you don't know well.

drills.prompt: Introduce yourself politely: 'I am a teacher.'

title: Addressing people with kinship terms (anh/chị/em/ông/bà/con/cháu)
explanationMd:
This is **the** central feature of Vietnamese. Instead of a neutral 'you', you address people with **family words** chosen by their **age and gender relative to you**:

- **anh** — a man slightly older than you (older brother)
- **chị** — a woman slightly older than you (older sister)
- **em** — a person younger than you (younger sibling); also how a younger person or a student is addressed
- **ông** — an old man / 'sir, grandfather'
- **bà** — an old woman / 'madam, grandmother'
- **con** — a child (a parent addressing their child)
- **cháu** — a young child speaking to a much older adult (grandchild / niece / nephew)

You judge age and pick the term. A waiter your age: **anh** or **chị**. A teenager: **em**. An elderly shopkeeper: **ông** / **bà**. Using the right term is basic politeness; getting it roughly right matters more than perfection.

examples.translation: Hello! (to a man a bit older than you)
Excuse me (older woman), may I ask. (you are younger)
Hello, sir. (a child/young person to an old man)

commonErrors.note: bạn (friend/peer) sounds textbook-flat with elders; use ông/bà for older people.

drills.prompt: Greet a woman slightly older than you.

title: How 'I' and 'you' shift with the relationship
explanationMd:
The kinship terms are **relative**, so the same person is different words depending on who is speaking. The pair moves together: whatever you call the other person, **you** usually call **yourself** the matching term.

- Talking **up** to someone older: they are **anh/chị/ông/bà**, and you become **em** or **cháu**. *Anh khỏe không? Em khỏe.* (Are you well? I'm well.)
- Talking **down** to someone younger: they are **em**, and you become **anh** or **chị**. *Em ăn chưa? Anh ăn rồi.* (Have you eaten? I have.)

So **em** can mean **I** (when speaking to an elder) or **you** (when speaking to a junior) — it depends on the direction. Two friends chatting might both call the older one **anh** and the younger one **em** throughout. When unsure, **tôi** (I) plus a kinship term for the other person is a safe mix.

examples.text: Anh ơi, em hỏi một chút.
examples.translation: Excuse me (older man), I have a quick question. (I = em)

examples.text: Em ơi, anh gọi món nhé.
examples.translation: Hey (younger one), I'll order. (I = anh, you = em)

examples.text: Bà ơi, cháu giúp bà nhé.
examples.translation: Grandma, I'll help you. (I = cháu, you = bà)

commonErrors.wrong: Anh khỏe không? Anh khỏe. (about yourself, to an elder)
commonErrors.note: If the other person is anh, you are em — don't call yourself anh too.

drills.prompt: To an older man, ask 'Have you eaten?' and answer 'I have.'
drills.answer: Anh ăn chưa? Em ăn rồi.

drills.prompt: To a younger person, say 'I'll help you.'
drills.answer: Anh giúp em nhé. (or Chị giúp em nhé.)

title: Classifiers and Number + Classifier + Noun
explanationMd:
To count or point at a noun, Vietnamese inserts a **classifier** (a measure word) between the number and the noun: **Number + Classifier + Noun**. Common classifiers:

- **cái** — general/inanimate things (bàn, ghế): *ba cái ghế* (three chairs)
- **con** — animals (and a few things like knives, rivers): *hai con chó* (two dogs)
- **chiếc** — vehicles and single items of a pair: *một chiếc xe* (one vehicle)
- **người** — people: *bốn người* (four people)
- **quả** (Northern) / trái (Southern) — fruit and round things: *năm quả cam* (five oranges)
- **cuốn / quyển** — books: *một cuốn sách* (one book)

With a bare number you must include the classifier: **hai con mèo** (two cats), not *hai mèo*. The classifier is also used with **này/kia/đó** to say 'this/that one' (see demonstratives).

examples.translation: There are four people in the room.

commonErrors.note: A number needs a classifier before the noun: hai con mèo.
Animals take con, not cái.

title: This and that: này, kia, đó
explanationMd:
Demonstratives come **after** the noun (or after the classifier + noun), the opposite of English:

- **này** — this (near the speaker): *cái này* (this one), *quyển sách này* (this book)
- **đó / ấy** — that (near the listener / already mentioned): *cái đó* (that one)
- **kia** — that over there (far from both): *cái kia* (that one over there)

Use them with a classifier to mean 'this/that one': **cái này** (this one), **con kia** (that animal over there). To ask 'which', use **nào**: *cái nào?* (which one?).

examples.text: Quyển sách kia là của tôi.
examples.translation: That book over there is mine.

commonErrors.note: The demonstrative follows the noun/classifier: cái này.
Order is classifier + noun + này: quyển sách này.

drills.answer: ngôi nhà kia (or cái nhà kia)

title: Past with đã
explanationMd: The verb never changes for tense. To mark a completed / past action, put **đã** **before the verb**: **Tôi đã ăn** (I ate / I have eaten). **đã** is often optional when the time is already clear from context or a time word (**hôm qua** = yesterday), so *Hôm qua tôi đi Hà Nội* (Yesterday I went to Hanoi) needs no đã. Use **đã** when you want to make 'already did' explicit. To mark completion you can also add **rồi** at the end (see the rồi/chưa entry), and the two often combine: **đã... rồi**.

commonErrors.note: No verb endings — mark past with đã before the verb.
đã goes before the verb, not after.

title: Progressive with đang
explanationMd: To say an action is **happening right now** (the -ing form), put **đang** **before the verb**: **Tôi đang ăn** (I am eating). It expresses an ongoing action at the moment of speaking, or at a reference time: *Lúc đó tôi đang ngủ* (I was sleeping at that time). Drop **đang** for general truths and habits — you would not use it in *Tôi thích cà phê* (I like coffee).

commonErrors.note: Don't use đang with states/preferences like thích (to like).
đang precedes the verb.

drills.prompt: Ask a friend 'What are you doing?'
drills.answer: Bạn đang làm gì? (or Em đang làm gì?)

title: Future with sẽ
explanationMd: For a **future** action, put **sẽ** **before the verb**: **Tôi sẽ đi** (I will go). Like đã, it is often optional when a future time word makes the meaning clear — *Ngày mai tôi đi Hà Nội* (Tomorrow I go/will go to Hanoi) is fine without sẽ. Use **sẽ** to state intentions and predictions: *Tôi sẽ gọi cho bạn* (I will call you).

examples.text: Ngày mai tôi sẽ gọi cho anh.

commonErrors.note: Use the Vietnamese marker sẽ before the verb.
sẽ comes before the verb.

drills.prompt: Say 'I will eat later.' (later = lát nữa)
Make future: 'Cô ấy học tiếng Anh.'
drills.answer: Cô ấy sẽ học tiếng Anh.

title: Just and about to: vừa / mới and sắp
explanationMd:
Two more time markers go **before the verb**:

- **vừa / mới** (or **vừa mới**) — 'just (now)', an action that happened a moment ago: *Tôi vừa ăn xong* (I just finished eating), *Cô ấy mới đến* (She just arrived).
- **sắp** — 'about to / soon', an action in the near future: *Tôi sắp đi* (I'm about to go), *Trời sắp mưa* (It's about to rain).

Think of them as a scale: **sắp** (about to) → now → **vừa/mới** (just did). **sắp** often pairs with **rồi** for 'almost / any moment now': *Sắp xong rồi* (Almost done).

examples.translation: The train is about to arrive.

commonErrors.wrong: Tôi sắp ăn xong. (meaning 'I just ate')
commonErrors.note: sắp = about to (future); vừa/mới = just did (past).

title: Already and not yet: rồi and chưa
explanationMd:
**rồi** = 'already / now' and goes at the **end** of the sentence to mark that something has happened or a state has changed: **Tôi ăn rồi** (I've already eaten), *Anh ấy đi rồi* (He's gone now). It often replaces or joins đã.

**chưa** = 'not yet' and goes **before the verb** to say something hasn't happened: **Tôi chưa ăn** (I haven't eaten yet). So the natural pair is **...rồi** (done) vs **chưa...** (not yet). The question 'have you ...yet?' uses **...chưa?** at the end, answered with **rồi** (yes) or **chưa** (not yet) — see the questions entry.

commonErrors.note: rồi goes at the end of the sentence.
For a statement, chưa goes before the verb; chưa at the end makes it a question.

title: Negation: không + verb
explanationMd: To make a verb or adjective negative, put **không** ('not') **directly before** it: **Tôi không ăn** (I don't eat / am not eating), **Tôi không mệt** (I'm not tired). It works for all tenses; combine with time markers as **không** + verb, e.g. *Tôi sẽ không đi* (I won't go), *Tôi chưa không...* is wrong — use **chưa** alone for 'not yet'. Note the same word **không** also means 'zero' and appears in the yes/no question frame **có...không** (see questions).

examples.text: Cô ấy không thích cà phê.
Hôm nay tôi không đi làm.
examples.translation: Today I'm not going to work.

commonErrors.note: không goes before the verb for negation (ăn không sounds like a question).
Adjectives take không directly, without là.

title: Negating nouns: không phải (là)
explanationMd:
You cannot negate the linking verb **là** with plain **không**. To say 'is not (a) ...' before a **noun**, use **không phải (là)**: **Tôi không phải là sinh viên** (I am not a student). The **là** is often dropped in speech: *Đây không phải điện thoại của tôi* (This isn't my phone). Compare:

- Before a **noun** → **không phải (là)**: *Cô ấy không phải là bác sĩ.* (She isn't a doctor.)
- Before a **verb/adjective** → plain **không**: *Cô ấy không đi.* / *Cô ấy không cao.*

**không phải** on its own also means 'that's not right / no'.

examples.text: Tôi không phải là người Việt.
Đây không phải là nhà tôi.
Không phải, đó là chị tôi.
examples.translation: No / That's not right, that's my older sister.

commonErrors.right: Tôi không phải là sinh viên.
commonErrors.note: Negate là with không phải, not không alone.
Before a verb use plain không; không phải is for noun predicates.

drills.answer: Tôi không phải là giáo viên.
Đây không phải là cà phê.

title: Yes/no questions: có...không and đã...chưa
explanationMd:
The main yes/no question frame wraps the verb: **có + verb + không?** → **Anh có ăn không?** (Do you eat / Are you eating?). Answer **có** (yes) or **không** (no). With adjectives you can drop có: *Anh khỏe không?* (Are you well?). For a plain question you may also just add **không?** to the end.

To ask 'have you ...yet?', use **đã + verb + chưa?** (đã is optional): **Anh (đã) ăn cơm chưa?** (Have you eaten yet?). Answer **rồi** (yes, already) or **chưa** (not yet). This đã...chưa frame is extremely common for checking on completed actions.

examples.text: Anh có nói tiếng Anh không?
Em ăn cơm chưa? — Rồi. / Chưa.
examples.translation: Have you eaten yet? — Yes (already). / Not yet.

commonErrors.note: The frame is có...không; don't add phải.

commonErrors.wrong: Anh ăn cơm không? (to ask 'have you eaten yet')
commonErrors.note: For 'yet?', end with chưa, not không.

drills.answer: Anh/Chị có uống cà phê không?

title: Tag questions: phải không and à
explanationMd: To turn a statement into a confirmation question ('right?', 'isn't it?'), add **phải không?** at the end: **Anh là người Hà Nội, phải không?** (You're from Hanoi, right?). In casual speech it shrinks to **phải không → không?** or even the very common **... à?**, a softer 'oh?/really?': *Anh đi Hà Nội à?* (Oh, you're going to Hanoi?). **à** signals mild surprise or friendly checking. Answer confirmations with **phải / đúng rồi** (that's right) or **không phải** (no).

examples.text: Anh là giáo viên, phải không?
Đây là nhà của chị, phải không? — Đúng rồi.
examples.translation: This is your house, right? — That's right.

commonErrors.wrong: Anh là giáo viên, không phải?
commonErrors.right: Anh là giáo viên, phải không?
commonErrors.note: The fixed tag is phải không, in that order.

drills.prompt: Add a tag: 'Chị là người Việt ___?'
Ask with surprise 'Oh, you're going now?'

title: Question words (gì, ai, đâu, nào, bao giờ, bao nhiêu, thế nào, tại sao)
explanationMd:
Question words usually stay **in the place of the answer** — you do not move them to the front like English:

- **gì** — what: *Anh làm gì?* (What do you do?)
- **ai** — who: *Ai đến?* (Who is coming?) / *Đó là ai?* (Who is that?)
- **đâu** — where: *Anh đi đâu?* (Where are you going?), *Ở đâu?* (Where?)
- **nào** — which (after a noun/classifier): *Cái nào?* (Which one?)
- **bao giờ / khi nào** — when: front = future (*Bao giờ anh đi?*), end = past (*Anh đi bao giờ?*)
- **bao nhiêu** — how much/many: *Bao nhiêu tiền?* (How much money?)
- **thế nào** — how / what like: *Món này thế nào?* (How's this dish?)
- **tại sao / sao** — why: *Tại sao anh buồn?* (Why are you sad?)

commonErrors.note: The question word stays in the answer's position, not at the front.
Don't add không to a wh-question; đâu already asks 'where'.

drills.answer: Đó là ai? (or Người đó là ai?)

title: 'To be' là vs. adjectives (no là before adjectives)
explanationMd: **là** links a subject to a **noun** ('X is a Y'): **Tôi là sinh viên** (I am a student), *Đây là cà phê* (This is coffee). But with **adjectives** Vietnamese uses **no 'to be' at all** — the adjective works like a verb on its own: **Tôi mệt** (I am tired), **Cà phê nóng** (The coffee is hot). Saying *Tôi là mệt* is a classic beginner mistake. Quick test: before a noun → use **là**; before an adjective → use nothing (and negate with **không** directly: *Tôi không mệt*).

examples.translation: I am very happy. (adjective → no là)

commonErrors.note: No là before an adjective.
Adjectives stand alone as the predicate — don't add là.

title: Adjectives follow the noun
explanationMd: When an adjective describes a noun, it comes **after** the noun, the reverse of English: **nhà đẹp** (house beautiful = a beautiful house), **áo đỏ** (shirt red = a red shirt), **cà phê nóng** (hot coffee). Intensifiers like **rất** (very) go **before** the adjective: *một ngôi nhà rất đẹp* (a very beautiful house). Full order inside a noun phrase is roughly: (classifier) + noun + adjective + demonstrative — *cái áo đỏ này* (this red shirt).

examples.text: Đây là một ngôi nhà đẹp.
Cái áo trắng này rất đẹp.
examples.translation: This white shirt is very beautiful.

commonErrors.note: Adjective comes after the noun: áo đỏ (red shirt).
Keep noun before adjective: nhà đẹp.

title: Comparatives: hơn, nhất, bằng, càng...càng
explanationMd:
Comparisons use small words after the adjective:

- **hơn** — 'more than': **A + adjective + hơn + B** → *Cà phê đắng hơn trà* (Coffee is more bitter than tea), *Anh ấy cao hơn tôi* (He is taller than me).
- **nhất** — 'the most' (superlative): **adjective + nhất** → *Phở này ngon nhất* (This phở is the most delicious).
- **bằng** — 'as ... as / equal to': *Tôi cao bằng anh* (I'm as tall as you).
- **càng ... càng ...** — 'the more ... the more ...': *Càng học càng thích* (The more I study, the more I like it).

There are no -er/-est endings; the adjective never changes.

examples.translation: This dish is the most delicious.
It gets hotter day by day (the more time passes, the hotter).

commonErrors.note: Use nhất alone for the superlative; don't mix with hơn.
Order is adjective + hơn: đắng hơn.

title: Possession with của
explanationMd: Possession is shown with **của** ('of'), placed between the thing and the owner: **thing + của + owner** → **sách của tôi** (book of me = my book), **nhà của cô ấy** (her house). The order is the reverse of English 'my book'. With close relationships (family, body parts) **của** is often dropped: *mẹ tôi* (my mother), *tay tôi* (my hand). To ask 'whose?', use **của ai**: *Cái này của ai?* (Whose is this?).

examples.text: Đây là điện thoại của tôi.
Xe của anh ấy màu đen.

commonErrors.note: Order is thing + của + owner: sách của tôi (my book).
của follows the possessed noun, not precedes it.

title: có: to have and there is/are
explanationMd:
**có** covers both **'to have'** and **'there is / there are'**:

- Possession: **Tôi có hai con mèo** (I have two cats).
- Existence: **Có** + noun → *Có một nhà hàng gần đây* (There's a restaurant nearby), *Ở đây có wifi không?* (Is there wifi here?).

Negate with **không có** (don't have / there isn't): *Tôi không có tiền* (I don't have money). Ask with the **có...không** frame: *Anh có xe không?* (Do you have a car?). Note **có** is also the 'yes' answer to those questions.

examples.text: Ở đây có nhà vệ sinh không?

commonErrors.note: Use có (have), not là, for possession.
Negate 'have' as không có, keeping có.

title: Modal verbs: muốn, có thể / được, phải, nên
explanationMd:
Modal ideas are separate helper words placed **before the main verb** (except **được**):

- **muốn** — want to: **Tôi muốn ăn phở** (I want to eat phở).
- **có thể** — can / be able to (before the verb): *Tôi có thể bơi* (I can swim). The word **được** means 'can / be allowed' and usually comes **after** the verb or phrase: *Tôi nói tiếng Việt được* (I can speak Vietnamese), *Ở đây hút thuốc được không?* (Can one smoke here?).
- **phải** — must / have to: *Tôi phải đi bây giờ* (I must go now).
- **nên** — should: *Anh nên nghỉ ngơi* (You should rest).

Negate before the verb ones with **không**: *không muốn* (don't want), *không nên* (shouldn't), *không phải* here would be confused with 'not be', so 'don't have to' is **không cần** (need not).

examples.text: Anh có thể giúp tôi không?

examples.translation: You should go to bed early.

commonErrors.note: Don't stack modals randomly; muốn + verb is enough for 'want to'.

commonErrors.right: Tôi nói tiếng Việt được. (or Tôi có thể nói tiếng Việt.)
commonErrors.note: được for 'can/able' usually comes after the verb phrase; use có thể before the verb.

title: Giving and 'for' with cho
explanationMd: **cho** means **'give'** and also **'to / for'** (the recipient). As a main verb: **Tôi cho anh ấy một quyển sách** (I give him a book) — order is **cho + recipient + thing**. As a preposition marking the recipient, it comes after the verb: *Tôi gọi cho anh* (I call you), *Mua cho tôi một cốc cà phê* (Buy me a coffee). In shops and restaurants **Cho tôi...** is the standard polite way to order: **Cho tôi một bát phở** (Give me / I'll have a bowl of phở).

examples.text: Cho tôi một cốc cà phê.
examples.translation: Give me / I'll have a cup of coffee.

commonErrors.wrong: Cho một cốc cà phê tôi.
commonErrors.right: Cho tôi một cốc cà phê.
commonErrors.note: Recipient comes right after cho: cho tôi + thing.
Use cho to mark the person you call/give to.

drills.prompt: Say 'I'll call you.' (to an older man)

title: Word order of time expressions
explanationMd: Time expressions (hôm qua, ngày mai, bây giờ, lúc 7 giờ...) are flexible but usually sit at the **start** of the sentence or right **after the subject** — not at the very end like English often does: **Hôm qua tôi đi Hà Nội** or **Tôi đi Hà Nội hôm qua** (both fine), with the front position most natural. Clock times use **lúc** ('at'): *Tôi dậy lúc 7 giờ* (I get up at 7). A rough order for a full sentence is **Time – Subject – (marker) – Verb – Object – Place**: *Ngày mai tôi sẽ gặp anh ở quán cà phê* (Tomorrow I'll meet you at the café).

examples.text: Ngày mai chúng ta đi biển nhé.
examples.translation: Tomorrow let's go to the beach.

commonErrors.note: A clock time with lúc normally follows the verb: dậy lúc 7 giờ.

commonErrors.wrong: Đi Hà Nội hôm qua tôi.
commonErrors.right: Hôm qua tôi đi Hà Nội.
commonErrors.note: Keep Subject–Verb order; put the time word at the front or after the subject.

drills.prompt: Say 'Tomorrow I will go to work.'
drills.answer: Ngày mai tôi sẽ đi làm.

title: Final particles for politeness and tone: nhé, nhỉ, ạ, đấy
explanationMd:
Small words at the **end** of a sentence carry tone, politeness, and attitude — they are essential for sounding natural:

- **nhé** — friendly suggestion, gentle request, or 'okay?': *Đi ăn nhé!* (Let's go eat, okay?), *Nhớ gọi cho mình nhé* (Remember to call me, okay).
- **nhỉ** — seeking agreement, 'isn't it? / right?': *Trời đẹp nhỉ!* (Nice weather, isn't it!).
- **ạ** — a respect particle added when speaking to elders/superiors; makes anything more polite: *Vâng ạ* (Yes (respectfully)), *Cảm ơn ạ* (Thank you, respectfully).
- **đấy** — draws attention / emphasis, 'you know / there': *Đắt đấy!* (That's expensive, you know!).

Adding **ạ** at the end of sentences with elders is one of the easiest ways to sound polite.

examples.translation: The weather's nice today, isn't it!

commonErrors.note: Add ạ with elders/superiors to sound properly polite.
For 'isn't it?' use nhỉ; nhé is for suggestions/requests.

drills.prompt: Suggest to a friend 'Let's eat, okay?'
drills.answer: Ăn thôi nhé! (or Đi ăn nhé!)

drills.prompt: Say 'Thank you' respectfully to an elder.
